page: Vultr voc-m-2c-16gb-200s-amd is a memory-optimized virtual server belonging to the Optimized Cloud Compute family. It features 2 shared vCPUs powered by an AMD EPYC Milan processor running at 2.0 GHz on the x86_64 architecture. The hardware configuration includes 16.0 GB of system memory, providing a high memory-to-core ratio of 8.0 GB per vCPU. Storage is bundled with 200 GB of local NVMe SSD capacity, and the instance includes one complimentary public IPv4 address. No dedicated GPU hardware is present.

Performance benchmarks reveal a mixed profile. The single-core stress-ng CPU performance is highly competitive, placing in the top tier (top 10%). However, multi-core stress-ng performance and large-block memory bandwidth write speeds fall into the average range. The shared CPU allocation introduces performance tradeoffs, as reflected in weak Passmark CPU scores and poor database operation throughput, which ranks in the bottom 10%. Static web serving throughput is also limited to 2.14 GB/sec.

Given the generous 16.0 GB memory allocation relative to its 2 vCPUs, this instance offers qualitative cost efficiency for memory-heavy workloads that do not require sustained, intensive multi-core CPU performance. It is well-suited for caching layers, lightweight memory-intensive applications, and moderate text processing or small-scale language model inference, while being less optimal for high-throughput database operations or intensive web serving.
description: Vultr voc-m-2c-16gb-200s-amd is a memory-optimized virtual server from the Optimized Cloud Compute family, featuring 2 shared AMD EPYC Milan vCPUs on the x86_64 architecture and 16.0 GB of RAM. The server is equipped with 200 GB of local NVMe SSD storage and includes one public IPv4 address. Benchmark results show top-tier single-core CPU performance, though multi-core operations and memory bandwidth remain average. Due to its shared CPU allocation, database operations and static web serving throughput are limited, placing in the bottom 10%. This instance is qualitatively cost-effective for memory-intensive workloads with moderate compute demands, such as caching layers, small-scale language model inference, and general-purpose memory-heavy applications.
